## HR Toolbox – Top 5 Challenges HR Professionals Face in Canadian Immigration

## HR Toolbox – Top 5 Challenges HR Professionals Face in Canadian Immigration
As more Canadian employers look beyond local talent pools, HR teams are increasingly involved in immigration processes. While there are many pathways available to bring in, or retain foreign talent, managing immigration is rarely straightforward. It often sits alongside tight hiring timelines and evolving business needs.
Based on our work with organizations across various industries, here are five of the most common areas where HR professionals tend to face difficulty, along with practical ways to approach them.

#### 1. Navigating a Complex and Evolving System
Canadian immigration is not a one-size-fits-all process. There are multiple programs — Labour Market Impact Assessments (LMIA), LMIA-exempt work permits, Express Entry, provincial programs — and each comes with its own rules and differences. For HR teams who do not deal with immigration every day, it can be difficult to know where to start, let alone keep up with policy changes. What worked for one employee or new hire, may not apply to the next.
Many organizations benefit from developing a foundational understanding of the main pathways while relying on trusted immigration lawyers for evolving requirements. Regular check-ins and updates can help HR teams stay informed without needing to track every policy change independently.

#### 2. Managing Timelines and Business Expectations
Immigration timelines do not always align with business needs. Processing times for work permits or LMIAs can vary widely depending on the pathway, role, and application volume. HR professionals often find themselves balancing internal pressure to onboard or retain talent quickly, with external processing realities that are beyond their control.
Setting realistic expectations early, both with hiring managers and foreign workers, can make a significant difference. Building buffer time into hiring plans, and retention strategies where available, can also help align timelines more effectively.

#### 3. Ensuring Accuracy and Consistency in Documentation
Immigration applications are detail-driven. The working conditions, position, salary, and supporting documents all need to be aligned. Even small inconsistencies can trigger delays or additional questions.
Working with an immigration law firm can add an extra layer of quality control. Immigration lawyers carefully review documentation to ensure consistency across documents that may otherwise be overlooked — whether it’s how duties are described, how compensation is structured, or how information is presented across forms. Having an external review can help ensure applications are aligned from the outset and throughout the entire immigration lifecycle.

#### 4. Balancing Compliance with Operational Flexibility
Once a foreign national employee is onboarded, or even when a new immigration document is issued, employers must comply with specific conditions tied to their work authorization. At the same time, business needs evolve — employees may be promoted, transferred, or assigned new responsibilities.HR professionals often need to balance these evolving business needs with immigration compliance requirements, which are not always flexible.
Integrating immigration considerations into broader HR processes — such as promotions, role changes, and relocations — can help ensure compliance is maintained without slowing down business operations. Proactively checking with your immigration lawyer before implementing changes can ensure employers remain compliant from an immigration perspective.

#### 5. Aligning Short-Term Hiring with Long-Term Retention
Many organizations initially focus on securing a work permit as quickly as possible. However, retaining international talent often requires a longer-term immigration strategy, particularly when employees are interested in permanent residence. HR professionals may find it challenging to align immediate hiring needs with longer-term planning, especially when managing large or fast-growing teams.
Considering permanent residence pathways early in the employment lifecycle can support retention. Aligning immigration planning with workforce strategy helps ensure continuity and strengthens employee engagement.
Canadian immigration offers employers a significant opportunity to access global talent, but it also requires thoughtful coordination and planning. With structured internal processes, clear communication, and the right strategic support, these challenges can be managed effectively. If your organization is navigating these issues, refining your approach can not only reduce administrative strain but also enhance your ability to attract and retain top international talent.

- Madison Kelly Madison Kelly is an Associate at Green and Spiegel LLP in Toronto. Madison assists clients with all aspects of immigration law in Canada and the United States. View Author Bio
